topic_groups.topic: Cancer Research, Oncology Care, and Cancer Prevention

articles.title: Certain demographic, birth, and parental characteristics may increase risk of early-onset colorectal cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study published in Cancer reports that birth weight, sex, ethnicity, and father's age may be associated with early-onset colorectal cancer risk. The findings point to demographic and early-life factors that could help researchers understand risk patterns in younger patients.

articles.title: Advice for physicians on supporting teens and young adults living with cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: A Canadian Medical Association Journal practice article offers guidance for clinicians caring for adolescents and young adults ages 15 to 39 who are living with or beyond cancer. The article emphasizes that this group has distinct medical, psychosocial, and survivorship needs.

articles.title: Only a third of eligible breast cancer patients receive a gene expression test
articles.summary_3_sentences: Research from the Netherlands reports that gene expression testing, which can help avoid unnecessary chemotherapy in some breast cancer patients, remains underused. Even after reimbursement, only about one in three eligible patients received the test.

articles.title: Mass spectrometry platform could shrink cancer drug discovery cycle to four hours
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers at the Purdue Institute for Cancer Research developed a mass spectrometry-based platform intended to accelerate early cancer drug discovery. The approach aims to shorten the process of identifying promising compounds to about four hours.

articles.title: Major review finds vaping likely causes lung and oral cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: A review concludes that nicotine vaping is likely to cause lung and oral cancers, drawing on evidence from human biomarkers, animal studies, and laboratory research. The authors argue that vaping should not be treated as harmless compared with smoking.

articles.title: This DNA repair gene went rogue and exposed a cancer weakness
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists report that excess production of the DNA repair gene EXO1 can damage DNA rather than protect it. The finding suggests that cancers with dysregulated EXO1 may have vulnerabilities related to faulty DNA repair.

articles.title: Combination Therapy Shows Promise for Hormone-Receptor Positive Breast Cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article discusses a combination therapy approach for hormone receptor-positive breast cancer. Details are limited, but it frames the work in the context of breast cancer's high prevalence in women in the United States.

articles.title: Scientists shut down cancer DNA repair to overcome drug resistance
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that UNI418 can interfere with cancer cells' ability to repair DNA damage. In combination with a PARP inhibitor, it helped resistant cancer cells respond again in laboratory work.

articles.title: GLP-1 Drugs Linked to 35% Lower Risk of Breast Cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports an association between GLP-1 drug use and lower breast cancer risk. It cites drugs such as Ozempic, Wegovy, and Mounjaro, but the summary does not establish causality.

articles.title: Cancer patients found a simple way to stay mentally sharp during chemotherapy
articles.summary_3_sentences: A trial found that cancer patients doing a home-based exercise program during chemotherapy had better attention and fewer perceived cognitive problems than a placebo group. Low-dose ibuprofen improved some cognitive measures, but its effects were less consistent.

articles.title: The secret reason some cancer treatments stop working
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified SLAMF6 as an immune-system brake that can weaken cancer-fighting T cells. Antibodies blocking SLAMF6 helped T cells attack tumors more effectively in mice.

articles.title: Combination Chemotherapy and Immunotherapy Treatment for Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article discusses combination chemotherapy and immunotherapy for non-small cell lung cancer. The summary provides limited detail but frames the approach as relevant to a common lung malignancy.

articles.title: National Cancer Survivors Day Celebrates Survivorship
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article marks National Cancer Survivors Day and highlights recognition of people with a history of cancer. It focuses on survivorship rather than a specific experimental finding.

articles.title: Ozempic and similar weight-loss drugs linked to 30% lower breast cancer risk
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large study found that women taking GLP-1 medications were about 30% less likely to develop breast cancer. Researchers described the findings as promising but not proof of prevention, and clinical trials are being planned.

articles.title: Scientists finally crack an "undruggable" pancreatic cancer target and nearly double survival
articles.summary_3_sentences: A clinical trial of daraxonrasib, a drug targeting KRAS-driven pancreatic cancer, reportedly nearly doubled survival in advanced disease. The article describes KRAS as a long-standing difficult cancer target.

articles.title: New Insights Into the Link Between Colon Cancer and a Bacterial Toxin
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article discusses research on links between colon cancer and a toxin produced by gut bacteria such as Bacteroides fragilis. Details are limited, but the focus is on microbial mechanisms that may contribute to disease.

articles.title: Novel Approach Engineers Bacteria to Deliver Viruses for Cancer Treatment
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists developed an approach using engineered bacteria to deliver viruses that can destroy tumor cells. The article presents this as an experimental cancer treatment strategy.

articles.title: Why cancer spreads more in middle age than in old age
articles.summary_3_sentences: A melanoma study in mice found that cancer spread was lowest in young animals, rose in middle-aged animals, and declined in very old animals. The researchers linked the pattern to an immune cell type that may help keep cancer dormant.

topic_groups.topic: Neurology, Dementia, Brain Aging, and Neurodevelopment

articles.title: Neuroimmune abnormalities may play a key role in fibromyalgia
articles.summary_3_sentences: A University of Barcelona study suggests that neuroimmune changes in the central nervous system may contribute to fibromyalgia. The work addresses possible biological mechanisms behind chronic widespread pain, fatigue, and cognitive symptoms.

articles.title: Tubulin prevents toxic brain protein clumps linked to Alzheimer's and Parkinson's
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers report that tubulin can redirect tau and alpha-synuclein away from harmful protein clumps inside brain cells. The finding is mechanistic and may inform future work on Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease biology.

articles.title: Scientists discover neurons must break their DNA to build the brain
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that newborn neurons can incur DNA double-strand breaks while migrating through tight spaces in the developing brain. The young brain appears able to repair this damage rapidly during normal development.

articles.title: Three genes may link six mental disorders through shared biomarkers
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study reports that three genes may act as shared biomarkers across several neuropsychiatric and neurodevelopmental conditions. The article notes possible overlap among schizophrenia, bipolar disorder, ADHD, and autism spectrum disorder.

articles.title: Dementia care: Re‑envisioning the role of music
articles.summary_3_sentences: A music therapist discusses how music is used in dementia care. The article frames music as more than a symptom-management tool, emphasizing meaning and quality of life for people with dementia.

articles.title: Scientists reprogram brain immune cells to fight Alzheimer's
articles.summary_3_sentences: A molecule called OLE restored brain immune cells to a more protective state in Alzheimer's models. The treatment reduced plaque buildup and improved memory in the models, but the evidence described is preclinical.

articles.title: Inflammation Worsens Brain Damage
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article describes how inflammation can worsen brain damage after traumatic brain injury. It links inflammatory processes to poorer cognitive outcomes following blows to the head.

articles.title: Scientists May Have Found What Really Triggers Alzheimer's Disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers suggest amyloid beta may contribute to Alzheimer's disease by interfering with tau, a protein important for neuron function. The article presents this as a possible trigger for later brain changes rather than definitive proof of disease causation.

articles.title: 10 surprising ways diabetes and dementia are connected
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reviews connections between diabetes and dementia, including glucose metabolism, insulin signaling, inflammation, and vascular damage. It also notes that some diabetes medications are being studied for possible effects on dementia risk.

articles.title: Copper drug clears toxic Alzheimer's proteins and restores memory
articles.summary_3_sentences: A copper-based compound improved clearance of toxic Alzheimer's-related proteins in laboratory experiments. The article reports reduced amyloid buildup and improved memory in experimental models, while noting the compound has prior human testing in other neurological conditions.

articles.title: Glucosamine Consumption is Linked to Increased Dementia Risk
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports an association between glucosamine use and increased dementia risk in health records from patients with neurological disorders. The summary indicates a possible link but does not establish causality.

articles.title: Your brain can keep improving into your 90s, study finds
articles.summary_3_sentences: A three-year study of nearly 4,000 adults ages 19 to 94 found improvements in several measures of brain health. Participants used brief daily brain-training activities, and gains were reported in thinking clarity, emotional well-being, and purpose.

articles.title: Learning a musical instrument in your 70s could help protect memory
articles.summary_3_sentences: A four-year study found that older adults who continued practicing a musical instrument maintained memory performance better than those who quit. The article also reports less age-related brain shrinkage in memory-related regions among continuing players.

articles.title: A hidden gene finally explains this rare neurological disorder
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified mutations in CD99L2 as a genetic cause of a rare movement disorder after studying nearly 3,000 patients. The gene, previously linked mainly to immunity, was shown to have a role in nerve-cell communication.

articles.title: Scientists discover a surprising cancer link to Alzheimer's disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that mutations associated with blood cancers may help generate inflammatory immune cells in the brain. The article suggests these changes could contribute to Alzheimer's disease and may open blood-based screening or treatment ideas.

articles.title: Scientists mapped every neural connection in a fruit fly and found a surprise
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers produced a connectome mapping every neural connection in an adult fruit fly's central nervous system. The work suggests complex behavior may arise from distributed local circuits rather than a single central controller.

articles.title: Popular joint supplement glucosamine linked to faster Alzheimer's progression
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study links glucosamine use to faster progression from mild cognitive impairment to Alzheimer's disease. The article reports a 25% higher likelihood of dementia among users and describes biological clues, but the summary does not prove causation.

articles.title: That ringing in your ears could be an early warning sign of hearing loss
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article explains that tinnitus and difficulty following conversation in noisy places can be early signs of hearing loss. It also notes possible effects on sleep, concentration, mood, falls, and cognitive decline.

articles.title: Scientists found a new Alzheimer's trigger and a drug that stops it
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified a new Alzheimer's-related target and developed an experimental compound to block a damaging process in brain cells. In mice, the treatment slowed nerve cell loss and reduced Alzheimer's-like changes.

articles.title: CBD Shows Early Promise for Alzheimer's
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports that cannabidiol reduced brain inflammation in a mouse model of Alzheimer's disease. The finding is preclinical and does not establish clinical benefit in humans.

articles.title: Brain scans reveal two distinct types of autism
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers combined brain scans from nearly 1,000 people with autism with data from genetically engineered mouse models. They identified possible hyperconnectivity and hypoconnectivity subtypes marked by different brain communication patterns.

articles.title: Evaluating CNS Anti-inflammatory Therapies with Human Brain Organoids
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article discusses using human brain organoids to evaluate anti-inflammatory therapies for central nervous system disorders. It focuses on pathways involving microglia, astrocytes, and cytokine signaling.

articles.title: Scientists found the hidden switch fueling alzheimer's brain inflammation
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scripps Research scientists identified a chemically altered form of STING that may keep brain immune responses overactive in Alzheimer's disease. The article links this mechanism to damage at nerve-cell connections.

articles.title: Protein traffic jams may explain aging, memory loss, and Alzheimer's
articles.summary_3_sentences: Stanford researchers studying turquoise killifish found that ribosomes can stall and collide with age. The resulting protein-production problems may contribute to faulty proteins and clumps linked to brain aging and Alzheimer's disease.

articles.title: CBD may slow Alzheimer's by calming the brain's immune system
articles.summary_3_sentences: In Alzheimer's mouse experiments, inhaled CBD reduced markers of neuroinflammation. The findings are preclinical and suggest a possible immune-related mechanism for future study.

articles.title: Human organoids reveal how to reverse "irreversible" nerve damage
articles.summary_3_sentences: Cambridge researchers created lab-grown brain-and-spinal-cord organoid systems that can send signals and trigger muscle contractions. They identified a gene network controlling loss of nerve regrowth capacity and found an existing hormone drug boosted nerve fiber regrowth in the model.

articles.title: Researchers block key protein that helps Parkinson's spread through the brain
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified GPNMB as a protein that may help Parkinson's-related damage spread between brain cells. Early experiments found that antibodies blocking GPNMB interrupted the toxic process between cells.

articles.title: Scientists create supercharged vitamin K that helps the brain heal itself
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists in Japan developed vitamin K-based compounds that were more effective than natural vitamin K at promoting neural stem cells to become neurons. The work is early-stage and may inform future research on neurodegenerative disease.

articles.title: USC scientists discover a hidden Alzheimer's trigger and a possible way to shut it down
articles.summary_3_sentences: USC researchers identified potential compounds targeting cPLA2, an enzyme linked to Alzheimer's-associated inflammation, especially in APOE4-related risk. The article presents the compounds as possible leads rather than established treatments.

topic_groups.topic: Mental Health, Behavior, Addiction, and Pain

articles.title: Performing under pressure? For athletes, it depends on 3 key things
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article discusses why some athletes perform well under pressure while others struggle. Details are limited, but it frames performance under stress as depending on three key factors.

articles.title: Clues to psilocybin's epigenetic effects on people with alcohol use disorder
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article describes research into psilocybin's epigenetic effects in people with alcohol use disorder. It places the work within growing interest in psychedelics as potential treatments for some mental health disorders.

articles.title: How dopamine modulates brain circuits of motivation in a behavioral addiction model
articles.summary_3_sentences: Kanazawa University researchers identified dopamine-driven mechanisms of motivation in a mouse model of behavioral addiction. The work is preclinical and may inform future treatment research.

articles.title: How people use music shapes their emotional experiences, new study finds
articles.summary_3_sentences: A University of Jyväskylä study reports that people's relationship with music influences whether they experience mixed emotions while listening. The findings were published in the Journal of Research in Personality.

articles.title: Nordic walking significantly reduces depression symptoms in as little as five weeks, trial finds
articles.summary_3_sentences: A trial found that Nordic walking significantly reduced depression symptoms in as little as five weeks. The activity uses poles and engages much of the body, making it more intensive than ordinary walking.

articles.title: The over‑50s are most likely to overdose. Here's how older people use drugs
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article highlights data showing that older Australians are increasingly dying from drug overdoses. It challenges assumptions that overdose risk mainly concerns younger people or festival drug use.

articles.title: Scientists found a cannabis compound that relieves pain without the high
articles.summary_3_sentences: Cannabis-derived terpenes reduced pain in mouse models of fibromyalgia and postsurgical pain. Geraniol showed particularly strong effects, and the compounds are described as non-psychoactive compared with THC.

articles.title: 64% of Cannabis Consumers Say It Helps Them Sleep
articles.summary_3_sentences: A survey from the American Academy of Sleep Medicine found that 64% of cannabis consumers reported it helps them sleep. The article discusses perceived sleep effects, not confirmed clinical efficacy.

articles.title: Ozempic and Wegovy linked to surprising drop in violent behavior
articles.summary_3_sentences: A Rutgers study suggests GLP-1 drugs may weaken the relationship between impulsive tendencies and violent behavior. The researchers caution that the findings do not prove cause and effect.

articles.title: A daily probiotic may help relieve depression and anxiety
articles.summary_3_sentences: A small clinical trial found that older adults with depression who took a daily probiotic alongside antidepressants had slightly greater improvements in depression and anxiety than placebo recipients. The study suggests a possible adjunctive role for probiotics but needs confirmation.

articles.title: Common plastic chemical linked to lifelong anxiety in new study
articles.summary_3_sentences: Male rats exposed early in life to the plasticizer DEHP showed higher anxiety-like behavior as adults. The study is animal-based and does not directly establish effects in humans.

articles.title: Scientists found an early depression clue hidden in children's eyes
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that depression and family history may influence how children attend to emotional facial expressions. Higher-risk children showed more attention to sadness, while lower-risk children showed less attention to happy expressions.

articles.title: Higher Tyrosine in Men Linked to Shorter Lifespan
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports an association between higher tyrosine levels or intake and shorter lifespan in men. It raises questions about over-the-counter tyrosine supplement use, but the summary does not establish causality.

articles.title: This popular brain supplement was linked to shorter lifespans in men
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large-scale study suggests men with higher levels of tyrosine may have shorter lifespans. The article notes that tyrosine is commonly marketed for focus and performance, but the finding is associative.

articles.title: Your brain was never designed for this much bad news
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article discusses how humans' attention to danger can be overwhelmed by constant exposure to negative news. Researchers recommend healthier habits around how and when people consume news rather than avoiding current events entirely.

articles.title: Genetic Analysis of Cocaine Addiction Points to the Liver
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers analyzed nearly 900 rats to map genetic factors related to cocaine addiction biology. The article reports that the findings point to the liver, but details are limited and the work is preclinical.

articles.title: Why middle age is becoming a breaking point in the U.S.
articles.summary_3_sentences: An international study found that middle-aged Americans are reporting more loneliness, depression, memory problems, and poorer health than earlier generations. Researchers cite financial strain, weaker social support, and chronic stress as possible contributors.

articles.title: Why grandparents matter more than ever for children's mental health
articles.summary_3_sentences: A child psychologist argues that grandparents can support children's mental health through listening, encouragement, and meaningful experiences. The article emphasizes relationships and purpose amid rising youth mental health concerns.

articles.title: New fentanyl vaccine blocks deadly overdoses before they start
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scripps Research developed an experimental vaccine designed to keep fentanyl-related drugs from reaching the brain. The vaccine targets a range of fentanyl analogs, but the article presents it as experimental rather than available care.

articles.title: World's largest opioid review finds they often don't work
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large review found that opioids for acute pain often provide only small, short-lived benefits and in some conditions performed no better than placebo. The review also found more side effects and warned that dependence can begin after short-term use.

articles.title: Study Examines the Genetic Link Between Cannabis Use and Psychosis
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article describes a Biological Psychiatry Global Open Science study on genetic links between cannabis use disorder and psychosis. Details are limited, but it focuses on biological pathways that may connect cannabis use and psychiatric outcomes.

articles.title: Popular GLP-1 weight-loss drugs linked to lower risks of addiction and overdose
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study of more than 600,000 U.S. veterans found GLP-1 drug use was associated with lower risks of substance use disorders and fewer overdose-related outcomes. The findings are observational and do not prove that the medications directly prevent addiction or overdose.

articles.title: Scientists reverse anxiety by fixing a tiny brain circuit
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified amygdala neurons involved in anxiety and social behavior. Restoring activity in this circuit reversed anxiety-like and social deficits in mice, making the finding preclinical.

articles.title: Arthritis Drug Shows Promise for Depression
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports that tocilizumab, an anti-inflammatory rheumatoid arthritis drug, showed promise for moderate-to-severe depression. Details are limited, but it supports interest in immune-targeted approaches to depression.

articles.title: New depression treatment targets the immune system instead of the brain
articles.summary_3_sentences: A small clinical trial found that an anti-inflammatory drug used for rheumatoid arthritis appeared to ease hard-to-treat depression. Participants also reported reduced fatigue and anxiety and improved quality of life.

articles.title: Scientists thought brain inflammation was driving long COVID but the scans told a different story
articles.summary_3_sentences: A brain imaging study found no evidence of widespread brain inflammation in patients with prolonged symptoms after COVID-19. More severe symptoms were associated with increased activity in brain regions involved in mood and emotion.

articles.title: Scientists "recharge" damaged nerves to ease chronic pain
articles.summary_3_sentences: Duke researchers found that supplying damaged nerves with healthy mitochondria may help restore nerve function and reduce chronic nerve pain. The work is experimental and focuses on a cellular mechanism rather than an established clinical treatment.

topic_groups.topic: Metabolic, Cardiovascular, Kidney, Endocrine, and GLP-1 Medicine

articles.title: One common fat may fuel type 2 diabetes while another helps fight it
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers report that palmitic acid may promote insulin resistance through inflammation, toxic fat buildup, and cellular stress. Oleic acid, abundant in olive oil, appeared to protect insulin function and counter some harmful effects in the described work.

articles.title: Most people seeking a kidney transplant in the US never reach the waitlist
articles.summary_3_sentences: A U.S. study found that many people with kidney failure who are referred for transplant never begin the evaluation process. Fewer than one-fifth complete assessment and reach the waitlist, highlighting barriers before listing.

articles.title: Later menarche may signal childhood factors linked to adult health risks
articles.summary_3_sentences: Research presented at ENDO 2026 links later menarche to childhood influences associated with adult health risks. Reported outcomes include tobacco use disorder and conditions affecting digestive, cardiovascular, bladder, joint, and brain health.

articles.title: Ozempic changed obesity treatment, but experts say the real revolution is next
articles.summary_3_sentences: Experts describe GLP-1 drugs as changing obesity care and anticipate broader treatment strategies. The article envisions combinations of medications, procedures, surgery, and precision medicine for long-term management.

articles.title: Researchers found 8 common food additives linked to high blood pressure and heart disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study of more than 112,000 people found that high intake of certain preservatives was associated with greater risks of hypertension and cardiovascular disease. Participants were followed for up to eight years, but the article describes associations rather than proof of causation.

articles.title: A surprising discovery reveals the kidney has a secret backup system
articles.summary_3_sentences: Mayo Clinic researchers identified a kidney water-conservation mechanism that can work independently of the hormone traditionally linked to the process. The finding emerged during tests of probenecid, which unexpectedly slowed cyst growth in polycystic kidney disease research.

articles.title: New procedure delivers lasting knee arthritis pain relief without surgery
articles.summary_3_sentences: A minimally invasive procedure blocking inflammation-driving blood vessels in the knee improved pain and function in osteoarthritis patients. Benefits reportedly lasted at least a year, offering a potential option before knee replacement.

articles.title: Calcium and Vitamin D Don't Prevent Bone Fractures
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports that calcium and vitamin D supplements, taken alone or together, did not protect older adults against fractures or falls. The summary provides limited details but aligns the finding with evidence on supplementation outcomes.

articles.title: Semaglutide Linked to Lower Risk of Bone Fracture
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports that semaglutide may be associated with lower fracture risk among people with type 2 diabetes. Details are limited, and the summary does not establish a causal protective effect.

articles.title: Most people who stop GLP-1 drugs like Ozempic eventually return
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that many people with type 2 diabetes who stop GLP-1 medications later restart them. Newer medications appeared to improve treatment persistence, while side effects remained a major reason for discontinuation.

articles.title: Semaglutide (Ozempic) linked to fewer bone fractures despite greater weight loss
articles.summary_3_sentences: A real-world study of nearly 60,000 adults with type 2 diabetes found semaglutide users had about 15% fewer fractures than people using other common weight-loss medications. The finding is observational, despite greater weight loss in the semaglutide group.

articles.title: New GLP-1 diabetes pill delivers major weight loss and blood sugar control
articles.summary_3_sentences: A major clinical trial found that an oral GLP-1 medication improved blood sugar control and produced weight loss in people with type 2 diabetes. The article highlights the potential convenience of a pill formulation.

articles.title: Millions take calcium and vitamin D for stronger bones. A major review finds little benefit
articles.summary_3_sentences: A review of nearly 154,000 people found little to no meaningful protection against fractures or falls from calcium, vitamin D, or their combination for most older adults. The article questions broad supplement use for bone protection without specific need.

articles.title: People taking GLP-1 weight loss drugs like Ozempic started moving less
articles.summary_3_sentences: Fitbit data suggested that people taking GLP-1 weight-loss drugs reduced daily steps and exercise after starting therapy. Researchers noted concerns about preserving muscle and strength during weight loss.

articles.title: Reversing prediabetes cuts risk of deadly heart problems by 58%
articles.summary_3_sentences: Long-term studies in the U.S. and China found that people who reversed prediabetes had lower cardiovascular risks. The article reports a 58% reduction in cardiovascular death or heart failure hospitalization and a 42% reduction in major heart events.

articles.title: A new guideline links care for heart, kidney and metabolic diseases
articles.summary_3_sentences: A new guideline frames heart disease, kidney disease, diabetes, and obesity as connected conditions under the umbrella of CKM syndrome. The approach encourages coordinated care across traditionally separate disease categories.

articles.title: Sleep and exercise may dampen genetic drivers of heart disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: Mouse data suggest that sleep and exercise may offset immune-cell mutations that promote atherosclerosis. The finding is preclinical and concerns lifestyle effects on genetic drivers of heart disease.

articles.title: A drug may help people on GLP-1 meds preserve muscle
articles.summary_3_sentences: A clinical trial found that an experimental antibody reduced lean-mass loss in people taking a GLP-1 drug. The article notes that whether this translates into better health outcomes remains unclear.

articles.title: Scientists discover why ozempic may not work for some people
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists identified genetic variants that may reduce response to GLP-1 drugs in type 2 diabetes. The article reports that about 10% of people may carry variants linked to poorer blood sugar target achievement in several trials.

articles.title: This new diabetes pill burns fat without the downsides of Ozempic
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists developed an experimental diabetes and obesity pill that activates skeletal muscle metabolism rather than reducing appetite. Early clinical results suggested it was safe and well tolerated while lowering blood sugar and increasing fat burning.

articles.title: Drug Delays Arthritis by up to 4 Years in High-Risk Patients
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports that abatacept delayed rheumatoid arthritis onset for up to four years in high-risk patients. The summary provides limited details but presents the drug as a preventive approach in selected patients.

articles.title: This drug delayed rheumatoid arthritis for years after treatment ended
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study found that one year of abatacept delayed rheumatoid arthritis onset by up to four years in people at high risk. Benefits persisted after treatment ended, suggesting a possible disease-interception strategy.

articles.title: Omega-3 fish oil shows promise against type 2 diabetes
articles.summary_3_sentences: In diabetic rats, omega-3 supplementation improved blood sugar, cholesterol, inflammation, and insulin resistance. The results are preclinical and do not establish efficacy in humans.

articles.title: New drug could finally stop deadly fatty liver disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: UC San Diego scientists reported that ION224, an experimental drug for MASH, targets a liver enzyme involved in fat buildup and inflammation. Clinical trial participants showed improvements in liver health without weight loss, according to the article.

articles.title: Scientists discover why Ozempic and Wegovy weight loss eventually plateaus
articles.summary_3_sentences: NIH researchers found that semaglutide produces different responses in appetite-controlling brain cells. The article suggests this may help explain variable responses and weight-loss plateaus with GLP-1 drugs.

articles.title: Beet juice lowers blood pressure in older adults in just 2 weeks
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study found that nitrate-rich beetroot juice lowered blood pressure in older adults over two weeks and altered oral bacteria. Younger adults did not show the same blood pressure reduction in the described study.

articles.title: Common heart drug taken by millions found useless -- and possibly dangerous
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large international study found no benefit from beta blockers after uncomplicated heart attacks in patients with normal heart function. The article reports a concerning signal of higher adverse outcomes among women taking the drugs.

topic_groups.topic: Nutrition, Supplements, Food Science, and Lifestyle Health

articles.title: How co-parenting reduces overall parental stress
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article discusses research and practice findings on how co-parenting and social support can reduce parental stress. It emphasizes the importance of shared caregiving and community support.

articles.title: Frozen fruit and canned veg are cheap, but are they as healthy as fresh food?
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article addresses whether frozen fruit and canned vegetables can be healthy alternatives to fresh produce. It frames the issue around affordability and the fact that many people do not eat enough fruits and vegetables.

articles.title: How a 45‑minute family exercise session could boost thinking skills
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article discusses how a 45-minute family exercise session may support thinking skills. It places the finding in the context of widespread insufficient physical activity among adults and children.

articles.title: Think you're eating healthy? You may be missing this heart-protecting nutrient
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers highlight flavanols as plant compounds linked to better heart health. The article notes that foods such as blackberries, plums, apples, broad beans, cherries, and green tea can raise flavanol intake.

articles.title: 90-120 Minutes of Strength Training Per Week Increases Lifespan
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports that 90 to 120 minutes of weekly strength training may be associated with longer lifespan. It cites research suggesting resistance exercise has significant health benefits.

articles.title: Scientists say most of what's in your food is still a mystery
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article describes "nutritional dark matter," referring to thousands of food chemicals not captured on standard nutrition labels. Researchers suggest these compounds may help explain links between diet, disease risk, and healthy aging.

articles.title: Scientists turned red lettuce green and something surprising happened
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers used genome editing to block red pigment production in lettuce. Other beneficial plant compounds accumulated while the lettuce continued growing normally, suggesting a way to customize crop nutrition.

articles.title: The secret behind smoother, better-tasting protein shakes
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers developed a whey protein manufacturing approach to reduce bitterness while preserving smooth texture. They traced unwanted taste to concentrated minerals and found a way to remove them.

articles.title: Scientists found a surprising problem with sugar-free diets
articles.summary_3_sentences: In mice, a sucrose-free low-fat diet worsened blood sugar control, inflammation, gut bacteria, and fatty liver markers compared with a diet containing some sucrose. The researchers caution against focusing only on eliminating sugar without considering overall balance and the microbiome.

articles.title: No, mastic gum won't reshape your jaw
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article explains that mastic gum may have some oral and gut-related benefits but will not reshape the jaw. It addresses a health claim and distinguishes plausible benefits from unsupported cosmetic effects.

articles.title: Can fasting fight gum disease? Scientists find surprising link
articles.summary_3_sentences: A small clinical study found that a low-calorie fasting-style diet reduced inflammation associated with gum disease. The findings suggest diet may influence periodontal health alongside oral hygiene.

articles.title: Scientists found the strength training sweet spot for a longer life
articles.summary_3_sentences: A 30-year study of more than 147,000 people found that 90 to 120 minutes of weekly strength training was linked to lower mortality risk. Combining strength and aerobic exercise was associated with even stronger benefits.

articles.title: Traditional Chinese Herb Shows Promise for Hair Loss
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports that Polygonum multiflorum, a root used in traditional Chinese medicine, may be useful for male and female pattern hair loss. Details are limited, and the article does not establish it as a proven therapy.

articles.title: Tea can improve your health and longevity, but the way you drink it matters
articles.summary_3_sentences: A review links tea consumption to possible benefits for heart disease, diabetes, cancer, cognitive decline, and muscle loss. The article cautions that bottled and bubble teas may contain additives that reduce health benefits.

articles.title: Ancient Chinese medicine could transform hair loss treatment
articles.summary_3_sentences: Research on Polygonum multiflorum suggests the traditional medicinal root may influence hormone pathways, hair-growth signaling, follicle protection, and scalp blood flow. The article presents it as a promising research area rather than an established hair-loss treatment.

articles.title: The supplements older adults actually need and the ones they don't
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article explains that older adults may benefit from supplements such as vitamin B12 or vitamin D when deficient, while protein intake may be especially important for strength. It also warns that unnecessary supplements can cause harm or interact with medications.

articles.title: This common amino acid helped mice survive deadly inflammation
articles.summary_3_sentences: In mice, methionine improved survival during severe infection and inflammatory conditions by boosting kidney filtration of inflammatory molecules. The finding is preclinical and does not prove benefit in humans.

articles.title: This tomato-soy juice reduced inflammation in just four weeks
articles.summary_3_sentences: A clinical study found that adults with obesity who drank tomato-soy juice daily for four weeks had reductions in several inflammatory blood proteins. A control tomato juice did not show the same effect.

articles.title: Caffeine reversed memory problems caused by sleep deprivation
articles.summary_3_sentences: Laboratory research found that sleep deprivation impaired a brain circuit involved in social memory. Caffeine restored communication in that pathway and reversed the memory deficit in the described experiments.

articles.title: Vitamin B12 and folate deficiencies linked to chronic fatigue
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers in Japan found that low vitamin B12 and folate levels may be associated with fatigue and reduced motivation in otherwise healthy people. The finding points to nutritional status as one possible contributor to chronic tiredness.

articles.title: Scientists discover hidden gut-brain circuit that triggers protein cravings
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers identified a gut-brain signaling network that drives animals to seek essential amino acids when protein is low. The work may inform understanding of appetite and nutrition but is not presented as a human treatment.

articles.title: Scientists say guava juice could make iron supplements work better
articles.summary_3_sentences: A review of 17 studies found that guava juice, especially with iron supplements, was linked to higher hemoglobin levels in women and teenage girls. Researchers attribute the effect partly to guava's high vitamin C content, which can support iron absorption.

articles.title: Surprising research reveals why you shouldn't add bananas to your smoothies
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that adding bananas to berry smoothies can reduce absorption of flavanols. The article highlights how food combinations can change nutrient availability.

articles.title: Scientists discover the perfect temperature to keep mangoes fresh much longer
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that storing mangoes at 54°F slowed ripening and helped preserve firmness, moisture, and antioxidants. The study also described internal stress-defense systems activated during cooler storage.

topic_groups.topic: Infectious Disease, Immunology, Public Health, and Environmental Health

articles.title: Single amino acid change may help viruses jump from bat to human
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article describes research on how a single amino acid change may help viruses cross from bats to humans. It places the work in the context of zoonotic spillover and pandemics such as COVID-19.

articles.title: How woodchips can help keep ticks off trails
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article discusses how woodchips may reduce tick exposure on trails. It is framed as a practical outdoor public health measure during warmer months.

articles.title: Smoking restrictions gain support across all 50 states over 30 years
articles.summary_3_sentences: UC San Diego researchers developed and validated a measure of changing U.S. social norms around cigarette smoking and secondhand smoke exposure over three decades. The study, published in BMJ Public Health, found growing support for smoking restrictions across all 50 states.

articles.title: New study attempts to reconcile conflicting findings on benefits vs. risks of alcohol consumption
articles.summary_3_sentences: A review discusses conflicting evidence on alcohol's health effects and identifies more than 60 diseases classified as fully attributable to alcohol consumption. It also notes that some alcohol-related damage may be slowed or reversed by cutting down or quitting.

articles.title: Ancient DNA reveals plague was already killing humans 5,500 years ago
articles.summary_3_sentences: Ancient DNA from Siberian hunter-gatherer cemeteries showed plague infections dating back 5,500 years. Researchers found early plague strains in nearly 40% of studied individuals and evidence of family-based outbreaks.

articles.title: New study explores potential cross-species spread of chronic wasting disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study found that chronic wasting disease prions can be present in animals without symptoms. The article notes there is no confirmed human risk but emphasizes monitoring because the disease can evolve and spread across species.

articles.title: Researchers Find a Cause of Inflammatory Bowel Disease
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports new insights into inflammatory bowel disease mechanisms. Details are limited, but it frames the work as identifying a cause or contributor to gastrointestinal inflammation.

articles.title: Investigating Escherichia albertii - An Emerging Pathogen
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article describes Escherichia albertii as an emerging pathogen linked to serious food poisoning outbreaks. It focuses on investigation of this organism and its public health relevance.

articles.title: Proteins Help Deadly Pathogen Evade Immunity
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article discusses proteins that help the bacterium causing louse-borne relapsing fever evade immune defenses. The disease is described as an acute bacterial infection with recurring high fever.

articles.title: Measles has no treatments. Changing that may not be easy
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article notes that vaccination remains the main tool against measles because there are no established treatments. Researchers are exploring antiviral drugs for people who are not vaccinated, but development may be difficult.

articles.title: The deadly tapeworm spreading across America has reached the Pacific Northwest
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers detected Echinococcus multilocularis in coyotes around Puget Sound, marking its first reported detection in wild animals on the U.S. West Coast. The parasite can cause severe, cancer-like disease in humans.

articles.title: A COVID-19 Preventive Strategy is Now FDA Approved
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports that a COVID-19 preventive strategy has received FDA approval. Details are limited, but it is framed as part of continuing efforts to prevent or treat COVID-19.

articles.title: A Natural Antifungal Mechanism is Revealed
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article describes a natural mechanism that helps defend against Aspergillus fumigatus, a common environmental fungus. It focuses on antifungal biology rather than a clinical intervention.

articles.title: AI-designed universal coronavirus vaccine passes first human trial
articles.summary_3_sentences: An AI-designed universal coronavirus vaccine was reported safe and well tolerated in its first human trial. It generated immune responses against multiple coronaviruses, including SARS-CoV-2, SARS, and related bat viruses.

articles.title: Curing Congo's Ebola outbreak is hampered by unknowns about the virus
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article discusses unanswered questions complicating response to Congo's Ebola outbreak. It notes that better information could guide treatments, outbreak prediction, and prevention of future outbreaks.

articles.title: Specific Immune Cells Found to Target a Range of Viral Strains
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports that specific T cells can target a range of viral strains. Details are limited, but the finding relates to immune recognition across viral variation.

articles.title: This common amino acid helped mice survive deadly inflammation
articles.summary_3_sentences: A Salk Institute study found that methionine improved survival in mice exposed to severe infection and inflammation. The proposed mechanism involved kidney filtration of inflammatory molecules, and the evidence is preclinical.

topic_groups.topic: Women's, Pediatric, Reproductive, and Developmental Health

articles.title: Down syndrome isn't a tragedy, but misinformation about it is
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article discusses how misinformation and negative medical framing around Down syndrome can shape prenatal counseling and family decisions. It argues that assumptions about limitations have contributed to stigma and exclusion.

articles.title: The mystery of the eye disease that can blind infants
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article examines retinopathy of prematurity, an eye disease that can cause blindness in infants if untreated. It highlights unexplained differences in treatment rates among Norwegian hospitals.

articles.title: Maternal weight may influence microbiota signaling in amniotic fluid
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study from the University of Oulu and Oulu University Hospital suggests maternal weight-related factors are reflected in microbiota-derived signaling particles in amniotic fluid. The finding indicates that microbiota signaling may extend into the fetal developmental environment.

articles.title: Fragile X deficits in mice respond to gene therapy
articles.summary_3_sentences: A gene therapy restored brain activity and improved behavior in a mouse model of fragile X syndrome by replacing a missing brain protein. The findings are preclinical and suggest a possible future strategy targeting the disorder's underlying cause.

articles.title: Humans may have hidden regenerative powers
articles.summary_3_sentences: Animal studies showed that a two-stage treatment redirected healing away from scarring and toward regrowth after amputation. Researchers reported restoration of bone, joints, ligaments, and tendons, but the evidence is preclinical.

articles.title: A Rare, Genetic Respiratory Disorder is Discovered
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports that variants in TMEM63B can cause a serious genetic respiratory disorder. Details are limited, but it describes identification of a rare inherited lung condition.

articles.title: Scientists discover gut bacteria that may help protect against autism and ADHD
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study suggests that epigenetic changes present at birth may influence gut microbiome development during infancy. Certain gene-microbe patterns were linked to early signs of autism and ADHD by age three.

topic_groups.topic: Regenerative Medicine, Musculoskeletal, Dermatology, Dental, and Sensory Health

articles.title: CT tissue images can now be virtually stained in 3D
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers report a technique for virtually staining CT tissue images in 3D. The approach could complement traditional pathology, which relies on stained thin tissue sections viewed under a microscope.

articles.title: First new US sunscreen ingredient since 1999 approved by FDA. A skin scientist explains how bemotrizinol works
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article explains FDA approval of bemotrizinol, the first new U.S. sunscreen ingredient since 1999. It discusses how the ingredient works as another option for sun protection.

articles.title: This giant tropical fruit could help reverse gum disease damage
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers developed a biomaterial from jackfruit latex, pomegranate peel, and simvastatin for severe gum disease. Early tests suggest it may fight infection and inflammation while supporting bone and tissue repair.

articles.title: Stanford scientists regrow lost cartilage and reverse arthritis in major breakthrough
articles.summary_3_sentences: A treatment blocking an aging-related protein restored cartilage in old mice and helped prevent arthritis after knee injuries. Human cartilage samples showed similar regenerative signals, but the animal data remain preclinical.

articles.title: Engineered hookworms could one day dispense drugs from inside your gut
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers genetically modified hookworms as an early step toward using parasites to deliver drugs inside the gut. The article presents the concept as experimental and not a current therapy.

articles.title: Traditional Chinese Herb Shows Promise for Hair Loss
articles.summary_3_sentences: Polygonum multiflorum is described as a traditional Chinese medicinal root being studied for pattern hair loss. The article suggests potential benefit but provides limited clinical detail.

articles.title: Ancient Chinese medicine could transform hair loss treatment
articles.summary_3_sentences: Studies suggest Polygonum multiflorum may affect hormonal pathways, hair-growth signals, follicle protection, and scalp blood flow. The article presents it as a research lead rather than proven treatment.

articles.title: Human organoids reveal how to reverse "irreversible" nerve damage
articles.summary_3_sentences: Lab-grown human brain-and-spinal-cord systems helped researchers identify a gene network controlling loss of nerve regrowth capacity. An existing hormone drug boosted nerve fiber regrowth in the organoid model.

articles.title: Popular anti-aging drug combo caused severe brain damage in mice
articles.summary_3_sentences: A mouse study found that an anti-aging drug combination caused severe myelin loss and changes resembling those seen in chemotherapy-related cognitive effects. The findings also offered clues relevant to multiple sclerosis biology.

articles.title: Scientists "recharge" damaged nerves to ease chronic pain
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that transferring healthy mitochondria to damaged nerves could revive nerve function and ease chronic pain in experimental work. The finding points to a mitochondrial mechanism for nerve repair.

topic_groups.topic: Basic Biology, Genetics, Evolution, and Aging

articles.title: Long-lived families reveal a rare genetic clue to healthy aging
articles.summary_3_sentences: A study of long-lived families identified rare genetic variants that may support healthier aging. One mutation was linked to reduced inflammation and potentially longer healthspan.

articles.title: Yale study finds nearly half of older adults improved with age
articles.summary_3_sentences: A long-term Yale study found that nearly half of adults over 65 improved physically, mentally, or both over time. More positive views of aging were associated with these improvements.

articles.title: Think human anatomy is finished? Scientists say think again
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports that scientists continue to identify overlooked structures and anatomical variation in the human body. It argues that anatomy remains an active field rather than a completed catalog.

articles.title: DNA time stamps reveal the strawberry's surprising origins
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers developed a method using transposable-element traces to reconstruct complex plant genome history. The technique showed that modern strawberries arose through multiple ancient genome-merging events.

articles.title: Novel, Protein-Chaperone Role for RNA is Revealed
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports a newly discovered role for messenger RNA as a protein chaperone. The finding expands understanding of RNA functions beyond carrying genetic instructions.

articles.title: Beneath our feet lies a fungal superhighway stretching 68 quadrillion miles
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers mapped underground fungal networks and estimated they stretch about 110 quadrillion kilometers. The article highlights their role in plant support, soil carbon storage, and climate regulation.

articles.title: Millipedes beat vertebrates to land by 80 million years
articles.summary_3_sentences: A new study suggests millipedes were present on land nearly 460 million years ago. The completed evolutionary family tree offers clues about early land ecosystems and millipede chemical defenses.

articles.title: Scientists discover a hidden cause of aging cells that can be reversed
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found that declining phosphatidylcholine levels may contribute to age-related mitochondrial dysfunction. Boosting the nutrient restored more youthful mitochondrial performance in aging organisms in the described work.

articles.title: Scientists propose a radical new theory for how life began on Earth
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers propose that mineral nanoparticles, or "nanozymes," may have catalyzed early chemical reactions leading to life's building blocks. The theory focuses on how lifeless matter may have transitioned toward living systems.

articles.title: Scientists Reveal a Novel Layer of Gene Regulation in the Heart
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article discusses alternative splicing as a molecular mechanism by which the heart adapts to changing conditions. It presents this as a newly described layer of cardiac gene regulation.

articles.title: Scientists discover inherited traits that break Mendel's Laws of genetics
articles.summary_3_sentences: A large mouse study found inherited traits transmitted through epigenetic changes that do not follow classic Mendelian rules. Researchers also reported the first known naturally occurring paramutation in a mammal.

articles.title: The ocean's health may depend on a tiny microbe inside fish
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers found evidence that gut bacteria in marine fish help produce calcium carbonate. The discovery suggests fish-microbe partnerships may influence ocean chemistry and carbon storage.

articles.title: The secret to pigeons' incredible navigation was hiding in their liver
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists identified iron-filled immune cells in pigeon livers that may act as magnetic sensors. Birds lacking these cells had trouble navigating under overcast skies, suggesting a role in magnetic-field guidance.

articles.title: Scientists say evolution may work differently than we thought
articles.summary_3_sentences: A University of Michigan study challenges the idea that most fixed genetic changes are neutral. Researchers found beneficial mutations may be more common than expected but often fail to spread because environments keep changing.

articles.title: Scientists discover ancient single-celled ancestors still live on in your blood
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers reconstructed the evolutionary history of human blood cells and linked them to single-celled ancestors from about 700 million years ago. The work explores how the immune system evolved from early life forms.

articles.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers studied cells that duplicate their DNA but fail to divide, leaving them with doubled genetic material. The article links this process to aging, cancer, and disease, and reports that not all such failures have the same outcome.

topic_groups.topic: Medical Technology, AI, Policy, and Health Systems

articles.title: As patients move through injury care, well-intentioned changes can backfire
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers argue that global injury-care policy should consider how entire healthcare systems work together. Improvements to one part of care may backfire if the broader system cannot support the change.

articles.title: The mystery of the eye disease that can blind infants
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article highlights unexplained variation in treatment for retinopathy of prematurity among Norwegian hospitals. It raises health-system questions about why infants at risk of blindness receive different levels of care.

articles.title: CT tissue images can now be virtually stained in 3D
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports that CT tissue images can be virtually stained in three dimensions. The method could provide a new way to view tissues without relying solely on conventional thin-section pathology.

articles.title: A classic brain test exposed AI's biggest weakness
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers tested leading AI models using a classic psychological attention task. The models performed well on short lists but their accuracy collapsed as the task became longer and more complex.

articles.title: AI scans 400,000 Reddit posts and finds hidden Ozempic side effects
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers used AI to analyze more than 400,000 Reddit posts about GLP-1 drugs and identified user-reported symptoms including menstrual changes, chills, and hot flashes. The article suggests social media analysis may help detect side-effect signals that trials can miss.

articles.title: A legendary golden fabric lost for 2,000 years has returned
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers in South Korea recreated sea silk using fibers from a clam cultivated in coastal waters. They found that its golden color comes from protein structures reflecting light rather than pigments or dyes.

articles.title: These tiny holes could change how the world cleans water
articles.summary_3_sentences: A nature-inspired membrane with uniform one-nanometer pores can separate molecules with high precision. The technology may reduce energy use and improve water reuse in industries such as pharmaceuticals and textiles.

articles.title: Scientists are seriously asking if bees and ChatGPT are conscious
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers are debating consciousness in insects and artificial intelligence by focusing on internal mechanisms rather than behavior alone. The article concludes that current AI is likely not conscious while leaving open questions about insects and future machines.

articles.title: Beluga whales keep switching mates and it may be saving their species
articles.summary_3_sentences: DNA analysis of more than 600 belugas in Alaska's Bristol Bay found that both males and females often have offspring with different partners over time. The article suggests this flexible mating pattern may support genetic diversity.

articles.title: A hidden pollutant is changing how the world's forests breathe
articles.summary_3_sentences: A global analysis found that nitrogen pollution can speed up or slow soil respiration depending on ecosystem conditions. The results point to tipping points that may affect forest carbon storage and climate resilience.

articles.title: Indigenous Andeans Had a Genetic Advantage to Eating Potatoes
articles.summary_3_sentences: The article reports that Indigenous Andean populations may have had genetic adaptations related to potato consumption. Details are limited, but it connects domestication of the potato with human genetic history.

articles.title: This newly discovered raptor may have hunted like a giant heron
articles.summary_3_sentences: Scientists described Kank australis, a 70-million-year-old raptor-like dinosaur from Patagonia. Its neck and vertebral features suggest it may have hunted fish in a manner similar to modern herons.

articles.title: A 100-year-old piano mystery has finally been solved
articles.summary_3_sentences: Researchers used high-speed sensing to show that pianists can alter tone color through subtle key movements. Listeners could hear differences caused by elite performers' touch.

articles.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles' lost crocodiles
articles.summary_3_sentences: DNA from museum specimens showed that vanished Seychelles crocodiles were not a unique species. They were an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely crossed the Indian Ocean.

excluded_items.title: From Atlas to Action: Going Deeper in the Next Era of Spatial Biology
